content: Inside every cell, mRNA acts like a messenger that carries important instructions from the cell’s control center to the part that makes proteins. Think of mRNA as a delivery note that tells the cell’s protein makers exactly what kind of protein to build. These proteins are essential because they help the cell perform many vital tasks, like repairing damage or fighting infections. The instructions on mRNA come from the DNA, which is like the cell’s master blueprint stored safely in the nucleus. Once made, mRNA leaves the nucleus and travels to the cell’s protein factories, called ribosomes, where the instructions are read. At the ribosomes, the mRNA’s code is translated into a chain of amino acids, which fold into a functional protein. This process is crucial because it allows cells to quickly respond to changes, like producing new proteins when needed. In summary, mRNA’s role as a messenger ensures that cells can efficiently create the proteins they need to keep us healthy and functioning.
